mechanism_narrative: Sustained acceleration in nonfarm payrolls (PAYEMS) over a 3-month period indicates robust economic momentum, leading to higher corporate earnings expectations and positive equity returns over the medium term.
evidence_refs: fact:PAYEMS has shown consistent growth through 2017 and into early 2018
failure_condition: A positive 3-month change in PAYEMS is followed by negative S&P 500 returns over the next 60 days in more than 50% of instances during a walk-forward test.

mechanism_narrative: When the year-over-year change in the Consumer Price Index (CPIAUCSL) exceeds 2.5%, it signals inflation is running hot, prompting expectations of aggressive Federal Reserve tightening, which negatively impacts equity valuations.
evidence_refs: fact:Inflation has been gradually trending up towards the Fed's target
failure_condition: SPX 20-day forward returns are not statistically significantly negative (p > 0.05) in months following a trigger event compared to non-triggered months.

mechanism_narrative: A combination of accelerating retail sales (RSAFS) and a rising unemployment rate (UNRATE) signals a deteriorating economic foundation where consumer spending is unsustainably high relative to labor market health, predicting a future drop in consumption and negative returns.
candidate_trigger: 3-mo change in UNRATE > 0.2 and 3-mo change in RSAFS > 1.0%
failure_condition: The interaction term between RSAFS and UNRATE changes in a regression predicting 60-day returns is not statistically significant and negative.

mechanism_narrative: In a late-cycle expansion, a sharp acceleration in industrial production (INDPRO) indicates potential economic overheating. This leads to input cost pressures and supply chain constraints, compressing corporate margins and causing negative equity returns.
evidence_refs: fact:The economy is widely considered to be in the later stages of its expansion as of early 2018
failure_condition: A strong positive momentum signal in INDPRO fails to be followed by negative 20-day S&P 500 returns more than 50% of the time during late-cycle periods.

mechanism_narrative: When the unemployment rate (UNRATE) falls below 4.2%, the labor market is exceptionally tight, leading to accelerating wage growth. This compresses corporate profit margins and forces the Federal Reserve to tighten policy, both of which are negative for equities.
evidence_refs: fact:The unemployment rate is currently 4.1%, meeting the trigger condition
failure_condition: During periods where UNRATE is below 4.2%, the average 60-day forward S&P 500 return is not statistically lower than periods where it is above 4.2%.

mechanism_narrative: A rising Federal Funds Rate (FEDFUNDS) directly increases the discount rate applied to corporate earnings and tightens financial conditions. This valuation and liquidity effect leads to negative S&P 500 returns over the subsequent month.
evidence_refs: fact:The Federal Reserve has been in a hiking cycle since December 2015
failure_condition: The rolling 36-month correlation between the 3-month change in FEDFUNDS and 20-day forward S&P 500 returns is positive for a sustained period (more than 6 months).

mechanism_narrative: Strong momentum in housing starts (HOUST) is a leading indicator of broad economic strength and consumer confidence, driving positive short-term equity returns as the market prices in higher growth.
failure_condition: The information coefficient of a 3-month momentum signal on HOUST for predicting 5-day S&P 500 returns is less than 0.02 over a 5-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: When inflation (CPIAUCSL) is accelerating while the labor market is simultaneously weakening (rising UNRATE), it signals a stagflationary environment. This combination is highly toxic for equities, as it implies falling real growth and an inability for the central bank to provide monetary support.
candidate_trigger: YoY % change in CPIAUCSL > 3-month change in UNRATE
failure_condition: Instances matching the trigger condition are not followed by negative 60-day S&P 500 returns in at least 60% of cases in a backtest.
